abstract.text: Integrating Swift with Zephyr RTOS for embedded systems development

inlineContent.text: is an open-source RTOS for embedded systems that is sponsored by the Linux Foundation. Since it depends on CMake primarily for its build system, it can easily be integrated to be used with Embedded Swift.

The following document outlines how to setup a Swift to Zephyr project for an emulated ARM Cortex M0, explaining a few key concepts along the way. For a complete working example on real hardware, however, refer to the
project that is compatible with nRF or other boards.
Embedded Swift is experimental. Public releases of Swift do not support Embedded Swift, yet. See

, but not all are supported by Embedded Swift. Please refer to the following table for an overview of Zephyr-supported architectures that are supported by Swift, along with the correct target triple to use:
Before setting up a Swift project that works with Zephyr, you need to setup dependencies and a Zephyr workspace as per the
. Regardless of your platform (macOS or Linux), ensure that you can build the blinky example without errors before starting with Swift integration:
revision of the Zephyr sources are checked out when calling
, which contains pre-release and development changes that may cause instability and changing APIs that are not desirable. To checkout a specific release version of Zephyr, use the following commands:

code: # For older versions of Zephyr (pre 4.1.0), use:

inlineContent.text: page for more information on current and LTS releases.
Once Zephyr is setup, the next step is to setup a project with the following files included:
These are the minimum required files in order to build a Zephyr project. By convention, source files should be placed in the src/ subdirectory, but this is not a hard requirement. Also,
is required even if it is empty, or the project will not build.

code: // Embedded Swift currently requires posix_memalign, but the C libraries in the
// Zephyr SDK do not provide it. Let's implement it and forward the calls to

inlineContent.text: Finally, add the following line to
setup is more involved and complex since target, compilation flags, and library linking must be specified for Swift. First, some initial setup and flags:

code: # Enable "wmo" as needed by Embedded Swift
# Create a new project called "SwiftZephyrProject" and enable "Swift" as a supported language

inlineContent.text: Next, set the compiler target to the arch you are building for. For this example we use
which we will compile for in a later step. If you are targeting a different architecture, see the
to see which target triple to use.

code: # Use the armv6m-none-none-eabi target triple for Swift

inlineContent.text: After setting the target triple, some additional additional Swift compiler flags need to be defined.

code: # Enable function sections to enable dead code stripping on elf
# Use software floating point operations matching GCC
# Use compacted C enums matching GCC

inlineContent.text: flag may need to change to
for ARM CPUs that support hard-float, such as the Cortex-M4F and Cortex-M7. This and the
flags are not required for non-ARM architectures such as Intel and RISC-V.
There are quite a few other Zephyr flags that can also be imported (optional) in order to get Zephyr include paths and flags such

code: # Import TOOLCHAIN_C_FLAGS from Zephyr as -Xcc flags
# Skip flags that are not known to swiftc
if(NOT is_imacro EQUAL -1 OR NOT is_mfp16 EQUAL -1)

inlineContent.text: Next, add the following block to automatically grab Zephyr compilation flags (such as
) and set them as Swift compiler definitions. This is required to successfully build Swift code that works with Zephyr:

code: # Add definitions from Zephyr to -Xcc flags

inlineContent.text: Finally, setup targets, libraries, and additional compile options:

code: # The Swift code providing "main" needs to be in an OBJECT library (instead of STATIC library) to make sure it actually gets linker.
# A STATIC library would get dropped from linking because Zephyr provides a default weak empty main definition.
# Copy include paths from C target to Swift target
# Link the Swift target into the primary target

inlineContent.text: To build the project, ensure that the Zephyr workspace is sourced first:
Run the following command to configure the project with CMake:

code: -- Build files have been written to: ~/SwiftZephyrProject/build

inlineContent.text: Then, the project can be built using

code: ~/zephyr-sdk-0.17.0/arm-zephyr-eabi/bin/../lib/gcc/arm-zephyr-eabi/12.2.0/../../../../arm-zephyr-eabi/bin/ld.bfd: warning: orphan section `.swift_modhash' from `app/libapp.a(Main.swift.obj)' being placed in section `.swift_modhash'

~/zephyr-sdk-0.17.0/arm-zephyr-eabi/bin/../lib/gcc/arm-zephyr-eabi/12.2.0/../../../../arm-zephyr-eabi/bin/ld.bfd: warning: orphan section `.swift_modhash' from `app/libapp.a(Main.swift.obj)' being placed in section `.swift_modhash'
Memory region         Used Size  Region Size  %age Used
Generating files from ~/SwiftZephyrProject/build/zephyr/zephyr.elf for board: qemu_cortex_m0

inlineContent.text: Finally, to run the example in the qemu emulator, use

code: [0/1] To exit from QEMU enter: 'CTRL+a, x'[QEMU] CPU: cortex-m0

inlineContent.text: Congrats, you now have a Swift project running using Zephyr!

Up to now we have setup a project that works perfectly fine when used with just CMake and Ninja. However, projects can also be integrated with West, which is the official CLI tool used for Zephyr projects. To use
file to the root of the project:

code: - cmsis  # required by the ARM port

inlineContent.text: It is recommended to set the
to a tagged version of Zephyr instead of always getting the main revision, which could have changing APIs.
Also, please note that depending on what architecture you are targeting, you may need to add more/different targets to the
, which is useful to get needed dependencies when compiling a project from a CI. See the
for an example of setting up a CI for Zephyr.

The default linker configuration for building a Zephyr project from CMake works well for simple projects, but it can be customized as needed. The following sections show off some useful ways to customize linking Zephyr projects for Swift.
When compiling Swift to Zephyr projects, you may see some warnings about orphaned sections from the linker, like

code: ~/zephyr-sdk-0.17.0/arm-zephyr-eabi/bin/../lib/gcc/arm-zephyr-eabi/12.2.0/../../../../arm-zephyr-eabi/bin/ld.bfd: warning: orphan section `.swift_modhash' from `app/libapp.a(Main.swift.obj)' being placed in section `.swift_modhash'

~/zephyr-sdk-0.17.0/arm-zephyr-eabi/bin/../lib/gcc/arm-zephyr-eabi/12.2.0/../../../../arm-zephyr-eabi/bin/ld.bfd: warning: orphan section `.swift_modhash' from `app/libapp.a(Main.swift.obj)' being placed in section `.swift_modhash

inlineContent.text: These types of warnings can be suppressed by passing a custom linker script to Zephyr that discards the sections, especially if they are not needed. For example, a script called
can be created at the root of the project with the following contents:
This can also help to reduce the size of the output elf/binary since unused sections are stripped out. Be careful what sections you strip out, however, as some sections may be required.
library from Swift to be linked into the Zephyr project. This is useful for linking unicode symbols when using strings. See
In order to add additional linker params, the CMake

code: # The code is using a String as a Dictionary key and thus require linking with libswiftUnicodeDataTables.a
# We compute the path where this file reside, taking into accout how the toolchain is referenced (Swiftly or TOOLCHAINS env variable).

inlineContent.text: Extra code is required to find the right paths where the
file is located, depending on how Swift is installed.
When this is built, depending on the target architecture, warnings may then be printed about 32-bit enums, like this:

code: ~/zephyr-sdk-0.17.0/arm-zephyr-eabi/bin/../lib/gcc/arm-zephyr-eabi/12.2.0/../../../../arm-zephyr-eabi/bin/ld.bfd: warning: ~/.local/share/swiftly/toolchains/6.1.0/usr/lib/swift/embedded/armv6m-none-none-eabi/libswiftUnicodeDataTables.a(UnicodeWord.cpp.o) uses 32-bit enums yet the output is to use variable-size enums; use of enum values across objects may fail

abstract.text: Embedded Swift is a compilation and language mode that enables development of baremetal, embedded and standalone software in Swift
Programming without an SDK for maximum control and minimal size
Get the tools needed to use Embedded Swift
Setting up a project that can seamlessly use C APIs from the Pico SDK.
Understand the common patterns and approaches for integrating Swift with existing embedded systems
How to enable full Unicode-compliant string support in Embedded Swift
